perturbation.original_text: 6. At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall promptly return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party in whatever medium, including any and all copies thereof. Such return (or, if authorized, destruction of Confidential Information) shall be confirmed in writing by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party in whatever medium, including any and all copies thereof within 90 days of the request. Such return (or, if authorized, destruction of Confidential Information) need not be confirmed in writing by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the return timeframe to 'within 90 days' and removing the requirement for written confirmation can be problematic under Pennsylvania law, which emphasizes the duty to safeguard confidential information and implies a reasonable timeframe that is much shorter than 90 days for returning confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract Laws in Pennsylvania, related to reasonable time and duty of good faith
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces a delay of 90 days for returning confidential information, which might be deemed unreasonable and violate the duty of good faith required in contract performance under Pennsylvania law, particularly if the information is sensitive or time-critical. Also, removing the requirement of written confirmation removes proof of compliance.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces inconsistencies regarding the return of confidential information. Specifically, it allows for a 90-day delay and removes the written confirmation requirement. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition states that a contradiction arises when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. While specific laws mandating immediate return or written confirmation aren't provided in the snippets, the perturbation explanation suggests that under Pennsylvania law, a 'reasonable timeframe' is implied, and 90 days might be considered unreasonable. Removing written confirmation also removes proof of compliance, which could be legally problematic.

perturbation.original_text: 1. The Recipient shall take responsible steps to ensure that all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party is kept confidential; provided, however, that such information may be disclosed to those employees or agents of the Recipient who have a need to know such information, or to such other party as may be agreed to by the Disclosing Party in writing, only if each such employee or party is informed by the Recipient of the confidential and proprietary nature of such information and of the confidentiality undertakings of the Recipient contained herein. The Recipient shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its employees or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. The Recipient shall take responsible steps to ensure that all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party is kept confidential; provided, however, that such information may be disclosed to those employees or agents of the Recipient who have a need to know such information, or to such other party as may be agreed to by the Disclosing Party verbally, even if each such employee or party is not informed by the Recipient of the confidential and proprietary nature of such information and of the confidentiality undertakings of the Recipient contained herein. The Recipient shall not be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its employees or agents.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the agreement to allow verbal consent for disclosure and removing the responsibility for employee breaches contradicts the principle of clearly defined and enforceable confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under PUTSA, reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets are critical. Allowing verbal consent and absolving the Recipient of responsibility for employee breaches undermines these efforts, potentially weakening trade secret protection available under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts Pennsylvania law regarding trade secrets. The original text required the Recipient to ensure confidential information was kept confidential, and held them responsible for employee breaches, aligning with the need to maintain secrecy as described in the Law Explanation. The changed text allows verbal consent for disclosure, removing the need to inform employees of confidentiality and absolving the Recipient of responsibility for employee breaches. This weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially violating 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304, which allows for damages for misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Each party agrees that it would be impossible or inadequate to measure and calculate the other party's damages from any breach of the covenants set forth in this Agreement. Accordingly, the parties agree that if either party breaches or threatens to breach any of such covenants, the non-breaching party will have available, in addition to any other right or remedy available, the right to obtain an injunction from a court of competent jurisdiction restraining such breach or threatened breach and to specific performance of any such provision of this Agreement. The parties further agree that no bond or other security shall be required in obtaining such equitable relief and each party hereby consents to the issuance of such injunction and to the ordering of specific performance.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Each party agrees that proving damages from any breach of the covenants set forth in this Agreement is possible and adequate. Accordingly, the parties agree that if either party breaches or threatens to breach any of such covenants, the non-breaching party will only have the right to monetary damages and will not be able to obtain an injunction from a court restraining such breach or threatened breach and will not have specific performance of any such provision of this Agreement. The parties further agree that a bond or other security shall be required in obtaining such monetary relief and each party does not consent to any other type of relief.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting remedies to only monetary damages and removing the possibility of injunctive relief while requiring a bond contradicts common equitable principles and legal standards in Pennsylvania, which often allow for injunctive relief in cases involving breaches of confidentiality agreements where monetary damages may be insufficient.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 1531
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania Rule of Civil Procedure 1531 addresses Injunctions and states the rules by which injunctions can be issued. The modified text that removes the ability to issue an injunction and also requires a bond, contradicts these rules and limits the remedies available to a party in a way that is not in line with Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 231 Pa. Code Rule 1531. Special Relief. Injunctions. Rule 1531. Special Relief. Injunctions. (a) A court shall issue a preliminary or special injunction only after written notice and hearing unless it appears to the satisfaction of the court that immediate and irreparable injury will be sustained before notice can be given or a hearing held, in which case the court may issue a preliminary or special injunction without a hearing or without notice. In determining whether a preliminary or special injunction should be granted and whether notice or a hearing should be required, the court may act on the basis of the averments of the pleadings or petition and may consider affidavits of parties or third persons or any other proof which the court may require. Official Note Section 601(b) of the Clean Streams Law, Act of June 22, 1937, P. L. 1987, 35 P. S. § § 691.601(b), relieves the Attorney General, the district attorney or the solicitor of a municipality from the requirement of bond in injunction proceedings. Section 7 of the Act of April 11, 1929, P. L. 488, No. 205, 3 P. S. § 297, relating to certified, inspected or registered seeds, similarly provides that the Attorney General shall not be required to give bond in injunction proceedings. (b) Except when the plaintiff is the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, a political subdivision or a department, board, commission, instrumentality or officer of the Commonwealth or of a political subdivision, a preliminary or special injunction shall be granted only if (1) the plaintiff files a bond in an amount fixed and with security approved by the court, naming the Commonwealth as obligee, conditioned that if the injunction is dissolved because improperly granted or for failure to hold a hearing, the plaintiff shall pay to any person injured all damages sustained by reason of granting the injunction and all legally taxable costs and fees, or (2) the plaintiff deposits with the prothonotary legal tender of the United States in an amount fixed by the court to
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for injunctive relief and specific performance, and waives the requirement for a bond. The changed text explicitly removes the possibility of injunctive relief and specific performance, restricts the remedy to monetary damages, and requires a bond. This contradicts Pennsylvania Rule of Civil Procedure 1531, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Rule 1531 discusses the conditions under which injunctions can be issued and, under certain conditions, requires a bond. The changed text eliminates the possibility of an injunction altogether and always requires a bond for monetary relief, directly contradicting the procedure outlined in Rule 1531, thus creating a legal contradiction, as it limits remedies available in a manner not in line with Pennsylvania law.

perturbation.original_text: 10. It is further understood and agreed that this Agreement may not be amended except by an instrument signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. This Agreement shall be governed by an interpreted in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, without giving effect to any conflict of law provisions. This Agreement shall become binding when any one or more counterparts hereof, individually or taken together, shall bear the signatures of PPI and Company. This Agreement may be executed in two or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed to be an original as against any party whose signature appears thereon, but all of which together shall constitute but one and the same instrument.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. It is further understood and agreed that this Agreement may be amended verbally. This Agreement shall not be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, and any conflict of law provisions will be given effect. This Agreement shall become binding when PPI and Company agree to its contents, irrespective of any signatures. This Agreement may only be executed in one counterpart.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing verbal amendments and disregarding Pennsylvania law contradicts fundamental contract law principles that require written amendments and clear choice of law clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Statute of Frauds, regarding contract modifications
perturbation.law_explanation: The Pennsylvania Statute of Frauds often requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable. Furthermore, verbal amendments are generally unenforceable when the original agreement requires written modifications, particularly in complex commercial agreements. Changing the governing law also undermines the parties' original intent and potentially creates uncertainty regarding the agreement's interpretation and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts standard contract law principles by allowing verbal amendments and changing the governing law. The original text explicitly requires written amendments, a standard practice to ensure clarity and enforceability in contracts. The changed text removes this requirement and introduces uncertainty by allowing verbal amendments, which are generally unenforceable, especially in complex commercial agreements. Additionally, the original agreement designates the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania as governing law, which provides a clear legal framework for interpretation and enforcement. The modified text removes this designation, creating ambiguity and potentially undermining the parties' original intent. The perturbation explanation and the law explanation highlight the unenforceability of verbal agreements and the importance of a designated governing law, reinforcing the existence of a legal contradiction.
